overview: Mono-Blue Spellementals is a tempo strategy that leverages spell-casting triggers to generate card advantage and board presence. By consistently playing instants and sorceries, the deck activates abilities on creatures like Sunderflock and Eddymurk Crab to outpace opponents. The goal is to stabilize early, control the board with efficient answers, and close the game through accumulated value and token generation.
win_conditions: Accumulating card advantage through spell-triggered draw effects
Generating a wide board of creature tokens to attack
Outlasting opponents through efficient removal and counterspells

keep_criteria: Hands with one-drops or early interaction to protect spell triggers
Multiple spell-casting outlets to activate Sunderflock and Eddymurk Crab
A mix of land and cheap instants/sorceries for turn-one or turn-two plays
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (or 1 land if on the play) and at least one 1-2 mana spell or key engine card like Eddymurk Crab or Three Steps Ahead.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking early interaction or spell density
Hands with too many lands or high-cost creatures without early plays
Hands that cannot cast spells on curve to trigger key abilities
Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early spells, multiple high-cost spells, or no way to interact with the board.

early_game: Play lands and cheap spells to establish board presence
Prioritize casting instants to trigger spell abilities immediately
Use early interaction to protect key creatures from removal
Turn 1: Play a land, cast a 1-mana cantrip or cheap spell to start the spell count.
Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-mana spell or deploy Eddymurk Crab.
Prioritize casting spells over attacking to build engine value.
If you have Three Steps Ahead, cast it early to dig for more spells or protect your board state.
mid_game: Maintain spell density to maximize token generation and card draw
Trade efficiently while preserving your trigger engines
Apply pressure with creatures while holding up answers for opponent's threats
Preserve mana for interaction and spell casting
avoid overcommitting to the board without protection.
Use cantrips to maintain hand size and ensure you always have spells to cast.
If the format utilizes the graveyard, protect it or leverage it for value.
Commit threats when you have interaction to protect them or when the board is clear.
Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal.
late_game: Leverage accumulated card advantage to outvalue opponents
Flood the board with tokens to overwhelm defenses
Use counterspells to shut down game-winning attempts while closing with your army
Sunderflock as a late-game threat or combo piece, or a high-spell-count win condition.
Use Three Steps Ahead to dig for the finisher or protection when needed.
Go all-in when you have enough spell count to make threats unblockable or unkillable, or when the opponent has exhausted their interaction.
Commit when you can safely attack or cast your win condition without fear of a sweepers.

key_cards.name: Three Steps Ahead
key_cards.notes: Cast as an instant to trigger spell abilities while refilling your hand and creating a 1/1 Spirit token for board presence.

key_cards.name: Sunderflock
key_cards.notes: Keep protected in the early game; each spell cast activates its ability to generate value or tokens, so prioritize casting other spells while it is on the battlefield.

key_cards.name: Eddymurk Crab
key_cards.notes: Functions as a secondary trigger source; cast spells freely to activate its ability, and use it to apply pressure or finish games through accumulated damage.

sequencing_tips: Cast instants before playing creatures to maximize trigger counts
Hold up interaction for opponent's key threats before committing to trigger engines
Prioritize drawing cards with Three Steps Ahead when your hand is low to maintain spell density
Use token generation to block multiple attackers while preserving your main creatures
Common mistake: Overcommitting to attacking instead of casting spells, which stalls your engine.
Unique interaction: Three Steps Ahead can be used to find protection or finishers, while Eddymurk Crab provides early value or spell acceleration.
Sunderflock is your late-game win condition.
Play around sweepers and counters, and always prioritize maintaining spell density in your deck.

matchups.advice: Prioritize early interaction and hold up counterspells for key threats; use spell triggers to generate blockers quickly.
Apply consistent pressure with tokens and creatures while using card draw to outlast their hand; avoid overcommitting to triggers if they have board wipes.
Race them by maximizing spell density and token generation; use interaction to disrupt their value creatures.
Cast spells in response to counters, use cheap spells to overwhelm counters, or employ protection. Split your threats across multiple turns to reduce the impact of single-target counters.
Use early interaction, discard, or counters to stop their big spells. Leverage cantrips to stay ahead in cards and pressure before they can stabilize.
Win the spell war by prioritizing card advantage, protecting your engine, and disrupting their spell count. Sideboard targeted removal and counters to break their momentum.

common_threats: Board wipes that clear your trigger engines
Counterspells that stop your key instants
Direct damage spells that bypass your blockers
High-toughness creatures that are hard to remove
Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and targeted removal.
Note when opponents hold up mana for interaction.
Track their graveyard if relevant, and monitor for combo pieces or win conditions that could end the game unexpectedly.
